> *"Most people use statistics like a drunk man uses a lamppost; more for support than illumination."*
>
> --- Andrew Lang

# Welcome

## BCB744 Biostatistics

The central task in this module is to align the biological question, the data collected, and the statistical model. This part of BCB744 develops the practical reasoning required to go from a biological question to a defensible statistical analysis. You will learn how to summarise data, choose appropriate inferential tools, fit and evaluate models, and present results within a reproducible workflow.

Use these module pages for timetable, assessment, and module-wide context:

-   [BCB744 Module Overview](../BCB744_index.qmd)
-   [Biostatistics Self-Assessment](../assessments/BCB744_Biostatistics_Self-Assessment.qmd)

# Introduction

In biology and ecology, statistics is part of scientific reasoning from the moment a question is framed. We use it to describe variation, quantify uncertainty, compare groups, assess associations, and build models that connect biological processes to observations.

In this section, I assume working familiarity with R, basic data handling, and graphical interpretation. If needed, review the [Introduction to R](../intro_r/index.qmd) materials before proceeding.

The emphasis throughout is on method selection, assumption checking, model interpretation, and clear scientific communication. As the series of tests develop, named tests are treated as parts of a broader statistical analysis framework rather than as isolated techniques.

# Statistics in the Scientific Process

In this section, I describe how statistical analysis fits into scientific workflow.

A scientific investigation in biology usually goes through the following stages:

1. observe a pattern or phenomenon;
2. formulate a clear biological question;
3. design a study that can produce relevant data;
4. identify the response, predictors, and experimental units;
5. anticipate alternative explanations and possible confounders;
6. choose an analysis that matches the design and data structure;
7. explore, analyse, and interpret the data; and
8. communicate the results clearly in words, figures, and tables.

So, statistics begins long before the final analysis, right at the beginning when we design our experiment or sampling campaign. If the design is flawed, no amount of later method massaging can fully rescue the inference.

The analytical steps in this workflow are made real through statistical tests and models. Some are simple and familiar, such as *t*-tests or ANOVA, while others are more complex but also more interesting regression models that allow several predictors, non-normal responses, dependence, or nonlinear structure.

# Key Concepts

The core ideas used throughout the module are:

-   **Statistical reasoning** links questions, data, and models.
-   **Inference** depends on the alignment of design, analysis, and interpretation.
-   **Models** provide formal descriptions of how responses vary with predictors.
-   **Explanation and prediction** are related but distinct analytical goals.
-   **Assumptions** are part of the analysis, not an afterthought.

# Core Principles

The practical rules that should guide analysis throughout the module are:

-   Define the biological question before choosing the method.
-   Understand how the data were generated and what the experimental units are.
-   Match the model to the process you are trying to explain.
-   Check assumptions explicitly and treat violations as meaningful information.
-   Distinguish between explanatory aims and predictive aims before interpreting coefficients or fit.

# What This Section Will Help You Do

By the end of this section, you should be able to:

-   summarise and visualise biological data to reveal structure, variability, and anomalies;
-   choose appropriate statistical methods for common biological questions;
-   fit and interpret linear and generalised models;
-   evaluate model assumptions, identify violations, and explain their consequences for inference;
-   integrate statistical analysis into a reproducible scientific workflow;
-   communicate your methods and results clearly.

# Section Structure

If you are new to statistics, follow the parts in order. If you have prior training, you may use early chapters as review before focusing on inference and modelling. The material is organised into five parts with each addressing a different type or stage of statistical application:

## Part I. Foundations

These are the fundamental concepts of data description, variation, and uncertainty:

-   [The Statistical Landscape](01-statistical-landscape.qmd)
-   [Summarising Biological Data](02-summarise-and-describe.qmd)
-   [Visualising Data](03-visualise.qmd)
-   [Distributions, Sampling, and Uncertainty](04-distributions-sampling-uncertainty.qmd)

## Part II. Inference

The formal inference tests for comparing groups and assessing associations is introduced:

-   [Statistical Inference](05-inference.qmd)
-   [Raw-Data Assumptions and Transformations](06-assumptions-and-transformations.qmd)
-   [*t*-Tests](07-t_tests.qmd)
-   [ANOVA](08-anova.qmd)
-   [Correlation and Association](09-correlation-and-association.qmd)
-   [Choosing the Right Test](10-test-selection.qmd)

## Part III. Modelling

This part is about models that explain variation or to predict from our data:

-   [Residuals and Model-Based Diagnostics](11-residuals-and-model-based-diagnostics.qmd)
-   [Simple Linear Regression](12-simple-linear-regression.qmd)
-   [Polynomial Regression](13-polynomial-regression.qmd)
-   [Multiple Regression and Model Specification](14-multiple-regression-and-model-specification.qmd)
-   [Interaction Effects](15-interaction-effects.qmd)
-   [Collinearity, Confounding, and Measurement Error](16-collinearity-confounding-measurement-error.qmd)
-   [Model Checking and Evaluation](17-model-checking-and-evaluation.qmd)

## Part IV. Extensions

Here I address non-independence, flexible model structures, and extended modelling frameworks --- these are beyond the basic toolkit:

-   [Pseudoreplication](18-pseudoreplication.qmd)
-   [Dependence and Mixed Models](19-dependence-and-mixed-models.qmd)
-   [Generalised Linear Models](20-generalised-linear-models.qmd)
-   [Generalised Additive Models](21-generalised-additive-models.qmd)
-   [Nonlinear Regression](22-nonlinear-regression.qmd)
-   [Quantile Regression](23-quantile-regression.qmd)
-   [Prediction and Explanation](24-prediction-and-explanation.qmd)
-   [Regularisation](25-regularisation.qmd)

## Part V. Reproducibility

Statistical analysis ties to transparent and repeatable scientific workflows (a part of reproducible research), which is discussed here:

-   [Reproducible Workflow](26-reproducible-workflow.qmd)
